sections.title: Social This Thursday 9th Jan
sections.content: First club night of 25 is this Thursday at the Horse and Farrier. Ginger Nomad coming to share some wisdom about flying XC.

sections.title: AGM Record of Meeting
sections.content:
Tam reports

Record of decisions, DHPC AGM 2024

Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club

Notes of Annual General Meeting 2024

Held at The Horse and Farrier, Otley. 8pm December 5th

Attendance

14 committee members present.

15 members present.

1. Previous Minutes and Actions

Previous minutes accepted.

Action point for committee to seek quote for auditing of accounts from accountant in Hawes: see points 4 and 6 below – action  complete.  

2. Adoption of Reports from Committee Members

Committee reports were made available on the members only section of the forum during November  and were published in the December edition of Skywords.

Resolved: Reports accepted.

3. Approval of Financial Statement / Balance Sheet

The Treasurer’s report was made available on the website on November 19th.

Key points Were reported at the meeting - not recorded in this version of ROD, but available on the forum.

Resolved: Report accepted (3 abstentions)

4. Appointment of Auditors

The committee remains of the view that appointing auditors would not be good value for money (£180 – £240), and are proposing removing this stipulation from the constitution. See item 6.

 

5. Approval of Subscription rates

Committee Proposals:

i. Membership fees remain the same (£25/£22.50 for prompt payment).

ii. Contributions to the Flying Fund to be maintained at 35%.

Resolved: Proposals carried (27 for, 1 against, 1 abstention).

iii) Until further notice, to adopt the HMRC approved mileage rates. For 2024/5 this would raise the mileage rate from 40p/mile to 45p/mile.

Resolved: Carried

6. Revision of Club Rules/Constitution

Committee proposal.

Remove Rule 5a subsection 4 which stipulates that the AGM agenda should include “Appointment of auditors”.

Amend Rule 4f which currently reads: The club’s financial year shall end on 30 September.

New proposed Rule 4f: The club’s financial year shall end on 30 September.  Accounts shall be opened for inspection by any member of the club at any reasonable time.

Resolved: Carried, 1 against , 3 abstentions.

 

7. Proposals from members

None.

8. Election of Officers

Committee Proposal:

Chairman – Martin Baxter

Secretary – Mark Morrison

Membership – Rahul Basu

Treasurer – Dave Bradwell

Safety – Clive Sury

Chief Coach – Pete Logan

Sites (N) – Tom Shuttleworth

Sites (S) – Stephen Craven  

Website – Alex Colbeck

Newsletter – Joseph Edmonds

Social – Stef Sykes

Library – Mike Barker

HG Rep – Trev Birkbeck

PG Comps – Ed Cleasby

Trophies – Richard Meek

First Aid – Rob Urselmann

Resolved: Proposal accepted.

9. Any other business

The Chairman noted the club’s thanks to Pete Darwood, Simon Tomlinson and Simon Browning, who stood down at the meeting, for their services to the club.

Meeting Closed: 8.50pm

 Next meeting: Thursday 4th December 2025.

MM

DHPC Secretary

6th December 2024

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Belated Merry Christmas: I hope that you had a good one.  Nothing to add to the minutes of the AGM and I didn’t get out flying last month, so I’ll resort to a couple of anecdotes from a previous life.

Christmas Day Morning 1993, during ‘The Troubles’ in Northern Ireland.  I was on an overnight duty as handling pilot of Lynx 5 based in a forward operating base at Bessbrook Mill near the border.  We unzipped our sleeping bags and stumbled down to the washrooms for a shave.  Just as we were lathering up the Quartermaster wandered in literally full of Christmas Spirit.  He was serving Gunfire; the military tradition on Christmas Day: a steaming hot mug of tea laced with rum.  We tried to explain that we were on flying duties and couldn’t touch a drop, but it fell on deaf ears.  We poured it down the sink once he’d left.

First task of the day was to take the Commanding Officer (CO) of the Infantry Battalion to visit his troops in the outstation at Crossmaglen (XMG).  As we wound up the engines, our own CO appeared.  More ‘aviation aware’, instead of rum he was offering a big jar of sweets.  We didn’t refuse those!

The threat was such that we operated as a pair with Lynx 7.  The sky was overcast and we flew fast at low-level, only climbing slightly and briefly to cross pylon lines.  The CO told us that he’d need an hour on the ground before needing pick-up.  That presented a bit of a dilemma because it was about an hour’s round trip back to Bessbrook Mill and we represented too much of a target sitting on the ground at XMG.  Fortunately, we managed to find a gap in the clouds and spent the next hour ‘playing’ above the clouds.  A surreal experience before returning to reality.

Another Christmas memory was back in North Yorkshire delivering Santa to a local primary school.  The teachers had all the kids safely behind a cordon; but as we came in to land on the school sports pitch, my co-pilot and I heard a strange noise and checked our instruments fearing a malfunction.  Despite the fact that we were both wearing helmets with integral headsets, the noise from 2 gas turbine engines only a couple of feet behind us was drowned out by screams from a hundred or so children excited to see Santa.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

First of all congratulations to Ged McCann who received his pilot rating after passing the exam in December.
Contact me (coaching@dhpc.org.uk) or any other coach (https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977) to see about getting your Pilot rating.

Slightly non traditionally I’m getting my report on 2024 out after the AGM.

Coaching changes

We currently have 21 coaches for a club of 218, a healthy number with a good geographic spread.

I was happy to add Rhian Ebrey, Joe Edmonds & Jason Perry who were endorsed Club Coaches.

It was tragic to lose Kev McLoughlin as both club member and a long-serving coach too. We're all thankful for the effort he put into the sport he loved.

Pete Balmforth stepped back from being a club coach too as he gave up flying. Thanks to him for his service as chief coach.

Clive Sury & Simon Browning stopped coaching too as their flying is not up to date. Thanks Clive & Simon; hopefully we'll see you return to the coaching list. Rhian's now moved to the Lakes so her coaching transfers up to the Penrith area.

New Pilots

Rhian Ebrey, Paul Rowntree, Alan Oliver, Ged McCann & Andrew Meth Cohn all gained pilot ratings this year. Congratulations and see you at the coast 🙂

On the subject of Cross Country, see the article I’ve written about CP XC based on the BHPA clarification of what is and isn’t insured for CP flights. 

Coaching Award

This year's coaching award goes to Joe Edmonds for the effort he's put into the dXCa/10-100 Club. Taking people out on several XC days. We had CPs doing first hops over he back and extended ridge runs. Appreciated Joe!

Coaching Activity

We had a coaching day on May 5th at Windbank. It wasn't very windy.

Martin Baxter and Chris Kay lead site familiarisation sessions on bad weather days at the Fly In. I lead gear discussion and harness setup with attendees there too. At 10-100 gatherings and regular flying days I, and other coaches have been conducting site briefings, giving XC advice and taking pilots through instrument and gear setup.

I created the DHPC Progress Telegram group to aid new CPs in getting together for flying, asking questions with coaches and generally feeling welcome to the club. It has 49 members, nine of whom are coaches or experienced pilots willing to help.

I have assisted Ian Currer & the BHPA in providing feedback about the online Pilot Exam version where it has been difficult for CPs with particular questions.

Next Year's Plans:

The Chief Coach can seem a bit admin oriented sometimes. I want to see if bringing some system to it can make it quicker to get through the comms etc. The website coaching section also needs an overhall which is on the winter todo list. We'll work out a way for Mike Barker to get his Paramotor Pilot Rating in the coming year. Alan Oliver is due to become a new club coach along with potentially Mike Barker and Jake Herbert too.

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Simon and Stephen delivered the Christmas Drinks to the southern sites farmers on 17 Dec, and were well received by all those they met (more than last year!).  Tom did the same for the northern farmers earlier in the month.

Total cost came to just over £1,000.  With the Farmers’ Dinner costing £1,600 that comes to about £2,600 in total.  I suspect that is a lot cheaper than paying site fees, and represents good value for money to secure access (and goodwill) to some of the best sites in the country.

The end of the shooting season tends to attract less ceremony than the start, mostly due to the weather; but come 1 Jan restrictions are lifted at a number of our flying sites.  Check the sites’ guide and traffic light colours on the website for further details.

sections.title: Wether Fell Gate
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

A number of you flew at Wether Fell on Fri 3 Jan, but the day was marred when Tom received a phone call from the farmer complaining about the gate being left open, with the risk of cows straying onto the road.  That’s a shame because the Allen family have always been great supporters of the club.  To be fair the Cam High Road is a Byway Open to All Traffic (BOAT) and is used by plenty of off-roaders as well as us.  

Please close this gate, even if you find it open.

Whilst the countryside code suggests that you should leave gates as you find them, that doesn’t really help if the person in front of you left it open.  If unsure it’s always better to close a gate behind you.  Tom has updated the site guide and reassured the farmers that you have all been briefed.  Because it’s a BOAT we’re keeping the option of buying some signs up our sleeves, but it might be a useful gesture of goodwill.

They also asked us not to use the parking space to the south on the wide grassy area.  Don’t be lazy: please drive to the proper parking area.

Please don’t be tempted to park or take-off from here.

sections.title: Club Pilots Not Insured when Flying Cross Country
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

Club Pilots flying XC are not insured

A couple of incidents have caused the BHPA to send out a clarification / re-statement that Club Pilots flying XC are not insured in the event of an incident. There is some leeway, as they also don’t want to stand in the way of pilot development. For formal events like the British Club Challenge or Lakes Charity Classic B comp or where a senior coach or instructor can take responsibility for a Club Pilot being briefed and able to fly a task, that is still insured.

For the X-Dales Hike and Fly Comp, Ed will probably make a judgement that it will be Pilot rating only from now on. The Northern Challenge Series has been updated too for Pilots only unless briefed by senior coach or instructor and signed off. https://www.xcflight.com/northern-challenge-series-2025/ An annoying procedure since it is specifically designed to expand growing pilot’s capabilities. The 10-100 club is in a similar position, briefings and a sign to fly are going to be necessary for CPs. The BHPA and coaches do want you to progress and will find ways to make it happen.  

I can appreciate that there’s significant scope for “what about x,y, or z situation” questions. The guidance is that CPs should keep their flight local - i.e. remain on or above the ridge line and generally within sight of the landing field. Long ridges that allow flights out of sight of a landing field are fine so long as your intention is to land back at the field. Staying within your capabilities and not getting “creative” with your interpretation of the guidance above is what will keep you insured, should there be an incident. I’ve dropped sections of the advice given out at the bottom.

In a way nothing has changed, other than events and the BHPA bringing what has always been the case back to the front of people’s minds - CPs should usually be working towards getting their Pilot rating and limiting their flights. I’ll send out more guidance if necessary and in time for the start of the ‘25 season. The club coaches have had a discussion about this already and will be discussing it more on a club coach course with the technical officers in a couple of week’s time.

The club has 74 CP rated pilots and I’ve had responses from half of you to find out what your intentions are for the coming year. Check your spam email, if you’ve not seen anything from me earlier in December. The Pilot rating is pretty easy:

  • Coaches sign off your tasks - https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/index.php?doc=Pilot_Tasks.pdf Get in touch and make use of the coaching team. Some of these need to be witnessed on the hill, some like height gain and hour flights can be signed off from your online tracklogs.

  • Study for your exam - https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/index.php?doc=Pilot_Syllabus.pdf Ask about revision sessions with your local coach or me (coaching@dhpc.org.uk). It’s a more than a few hours study but not too demanding.  

  • Any coach can invigilate an exam - book in with them or me. Ed Cleasby runs exams in the west Dales area so we have reasonable coverage of the membership. Your coaches are here: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/coaching and here: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977  And there’s a mock exam here: https://www.bhpa.co.uk/safety/mock_exams/ 

May your lift be steady and high for the ‘25 flying season.

Read more here:

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/safety/newsletters/index.php?doc=ic30_Dec_2024.pdf

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/safety/newsletters/index.php?doc=ic30a_Jan_2025.pdf

sections.title: BHPA Membership Numbers
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Numbers continue to slide, possibly due to the poor weather last year.  What implications does this have for me?  Some overheads, like Skywings, will reduce but others, like insurance, won’t.  Fewer members generally means higher membership fees 🙁

subsections.title: Announcing the DHPC Reserve Repack Day - SATURDAY 18th January 2025
subsections.content:
When?

- SATURDAY 18th January 2025 with a choice of two sessions: 10:00 am or 1pm

Where?

 - Ilkley Grammar School, LS29 8TR.   B Hall – Cowpasture Road entrance

Who’s the repack for?

 - All pilots of all qualification levels and experience: hang gliders; paramotors; speedwings; paragliders.  Plus the event is open to both DHPC members and non-members

Who’s doing it?

 - Guy Richardson, Ginger Nomad will be taking the lead with assistance from 2 other qualified re-packers

Why should I do this?

A properly packed and fitted reserve can open within 30 - 50m of being thrown. Most manufacturers recommend repacking every six months (some as frequently as 90 days). There are several reasons for this:

  • Once the reserve is packed, it can absorb moisture from being left in damp air conditions, not just wet from rain. The moisture will not get back out again easily and can cause damage to the strength of fabric and lines as well as causing the fabric of the canopy to stick together.  
  • The reserve packed in the harness gets squashed, especially if the harness is the type that the pilot is likely to sit on whilst waiting for a launch or Skywords – Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club – Jan/Feb 2022 26 having their sandwiches. The effect of this is to almost iron creases into the reserve which have been shown to make the deployment slower, as the airstream takes longer to get between the leaves of the canopy to allow it to open.  
  • After about a year, the rubber bands that hold everything together long enough to deploy properly, can decay and need checking.  
  • Any velcro in the system needs to be checked because if left for a long time it can get “welded” together, and it takes great strength to pull the reserve out. It has also happened that the velcro on the side holders for the bridle on the harness did not open.  
  • A reserve can also be difficult or sometimes impossible to throw due to incorrect fitting to the harness
  • Finally - Can you refit your reserve if it accidentally falls out? It really is simple once you know how. If you cannot refit it safely then at best you could lose a day’s flying and at worst

What’s involved?

Bring your reserve and harness and after a brief presentation from Guy, working in pairs with limited supervision and guidance from licensed BHPA re-packers on how to repack your reserve. Please note that this is not a certified repack service.  Should you require this, you can arrange this with Guy Richardson directly who can collect/ deliver your reserve to you on the day, providing this certified service outside of the day itself.

What do I need to bring with me?

  1. Your reserve and harness
  2. The printed reserve instructions for your make and model of reserve parachute.  It is best to have a printed version as reading on a phone has proved challenging in the past.  These should be available from the manufacturer’s website.
  3. A bit of string, paracord or something suitable for the packing loops on your reserve (if appropriate to your model) and also a bit of paraglider line or strimmer cord to assist with the pins when putting it in the harness.
  4. Suitable rubber bands to pack the lines. These should be available from Guy Richardson on the day.  However, the best option is to source the same rubber bands used by the manufacturer of your rescue chute - perhaps drop the manufacturer an email to see if they can point you in the right direction. Failing that, you could search the internet for size 8 rubber bands (not elastic bands). They should have as high a rubber content as possible - over 80% is good. One source is UK Airsports (note: I am not affiliated with UK Airsports).
  5.  Warm clothing - the B Hall may be cold.

Are there any special offers on equipment on the day?

Yes - Guy will offer discounts on reserves and other equipment. If you have a reserve that you think may need replacing, one option is to contact Guy in advance and he can bring it with him.  His contact details are:

Ginger Nomad & Ginger Air – Full of Ginger Outdoor Goodness – Call us on +44 114 392 0032

How much does the repack day cost?

£19.67 - the money goes towards the hall hire, Guy and his assistants expenses, and Eventbrite’s booking fee.

How do I book a place?

https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/1109167583099?aff=oddtdtcreator

Please keep to your morning or afternoon session.  Turning up at the wrong time or, without booking may lead to being disappointed.

Anything else I need to know?

Yes, three more items:

  1.  There are limited places for each session.  Therefore, it pays to book early to secure your place
  2. Please ensure that you arrive on time for the session you book and leave after 2 ½ hours to allow either time for the next session or for us to clear the hall at the end of the day.  If you would like to ask me a question direct then please feel welcome to email me - safety@dhpc.org.uk
  3. Finally, it’s good fun.  A great way to get to know others in the club, learn more about your equipment and be more confident about how the reserve functions.

Happy repacking,

Clive Sury.

sections.title: The 45 degree rule…
sections.content:
Mike Barker reports...

A tale from the dark side (PPG). It was Friday the 13th and a few people were heading to Wetherfell…I was undecided as the weather also looked great for PPG so I opted for the latter as my local field is just 5 minutes away. I did a bit of ground handling and watched a few guys going XC passing over Pateley Bridge. I decided to go for a local flight to the Coldstone cut.

The sky was blue with some clouds but nothing to worry me when I took off. Heading over Pateley I noticed my climb rate suddenly increasing, not something I’m used to with my Paramotor unless I go full power. Realising I’d entered a strong thermal, I eased off the power, still the Vario was beeping at me. I then went to complete idle and light breaks just to feel the wing, which would normally give me a high sink rate with the extra 30kg on my back when compared to paragliding, but no, I was still going up! I glanced down to see the Vario +5 m/s! At this point I decided to do a 180 and fly back to where I came from… hitting a bit of turbulence as I left the thermal but thankfully now going down! Phew…I headed for the landing field and made a safe landing.

When I landed my other half showed me some pictures of my flight…then I realised what I had done! Not paid enough attention to the clouds ahead of me and flew underneath a rather large cumulus cloud!  I’m about 2 O’clock in the cloud towards the centre!

 

Was I being a bit melodramatic? Is +5 m/s a concern? Would it have been much higher if I was paragliding without the extra weight of my engine? Did I do the right thing? Should I have tried big ears? Lots to think about and digest once home. In my post flight analysis, I came across 2 pieces of advice; Preventative the 45-degree rule, and Reactionary fly away in the direction you came from.  I’m glad I did the latter, but could I have done more…The 45-degree rule for paragliding is a safety tip to help avoid cloud suck by ensuring the edge of a cloud is always more than 45 degrees above the horizon. Something I’ll certainly remember now, and a great example of it here from Paragliding XC stories.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jjcoxkVMK1o

Researching emergency descent techniques, (other than what we are taught in CP), things I would at this stage be uncomfortable with, has pushed me to booking a SIV course this Easter…not something I am entirely looking forward to, (a mix of excitement and intrepidation) but something I now feel I need in terms of valuable experience to help me manage these situations with more confidence in the future.

 

I decided to ask AI to improve my write-up; instead, it answered my questions…here’s its response

It sounds like you had an intense and eye-opening experience while paramotoring on that Friday the 13th! Let's break down your thoughts and address the concerns you've raised.

1. Was +5 m/s a concern?

A climb rate of +5 m/s (meters per second) is significant, especially when you're flying with a paramotor. The general rule of thumb is that strong thermals can push you upwards at varying rates, and anything above +3 m/s is typically considered a strong thermal. If you hadn’t been aware of it, this could have been very concerning, particularly with the added weight of your engine, which makes it harder to manage climb rates compared to paragliding alone. However, being aware of this and choosing to exit the thermal in time shows you made a smart decision.

2. Would it have been much higher if I was paragliding without the extra weight of my engine?

Yes, if you had been paragliding without the engine, the climb rate would likely have been higher. Paragliders are much lighter than paramotors, so they can more easily take advantage of strong thermals. Your paramotor adds extra weight, which helps you control the climb rate to some degree, but also means that thermals will push you up more slowly. Without the engine, you'd have been more susceptible to climbing faster and might have needed to make decisions even sooner to avoid cloud suck or entering a dangerous situation.

3. Did I do the right thing?

Absolutely! Realizing you were in a strong thermal and choosing to turn back rather than continue upward was a wise move. When you’re in an unknown or potentially dangerous situation like flying near large cumulus clouds, it’s best to err on the side of caution. The turbulence you encountered as you left the thermal could have been a result of passing through the edge of the cloud, which can be tricky. Deciding to head back to the landing field and making a safe landing was the correct course of action.

4. Should I have tried Big Ears?

Big Ears is a technique used to quickly descend in a controlled manner by collapsing the wing’s trailing edges, which can be helpful in certain situations to speed up your descent. However, when you're already in a thermal, Big Ears might not always be the best option, especially since it could bring you down too quickly, possibly putting you in an unstable air mass or causing a rough landing. Since you were already experiencing turbulence and deciding to turn back was working in your favor, trying Big Ears might not have been necessary in this case. You were smart to focus on managing the situation and heading back to safety.

5. The 45-Degree Rule

The 45-degree rule for paragliding is a crucial safety tip to avoid the risk of cloud suck. This rule suggests that you should always ensure that the cloud you are under is at least 45 degrees above the horizon to avoid being sucked into it. From your description, it seems you flew too close to the center of a large cumulus cloud, which could have posed a serious risk. Had you been more aware of your surroundings, you might have avoided this situation, but now you've learned a valuable lesson that will guide your future flights.

6. SIV Course

Booking an SIV (Simulation of Incidents in Flight) course is an excellent step to improve your skills and confidence. It will allow you to practice emergency maneuvers, such as recovering from collapses and spirals, in a controlled environment with instructors by your side. While it’s understandable to feel a mix of excitement and apprehension, it will be invaluable experience for handling situations like the one you encountered. You'll feel more prepared and confident when faced with challenging conditions.

Conclusion

In summary, you did a great job reacting to the situation. The 45-degree rule and your decision to fly away from the thermal were both wise choices. Your experience highlights the importance of constant awareness and preparedness when paramotoring or paragliding, and it’s clear that you’re committed to improving your skills and safety. Keep up the thoughtful approach, and good luck with your SIV course! You'll come away from it with greater confidence and techniques that will serve you well in future flights.

sections.title: Comps ‘25
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby reports

Apologies for this being very brief, I‘m squeezing it in before the deadline and mainly as a reminder.

Aside from paying out big bucks and travelling abroad to one of the many competitions available for all levels, but mostly Pilot rated and above, it’s worth considering what’s on offer on UK soil and locally.

BCC (British Club Championships). 

Up until about 10 years ago (it may be longer) all the northern clubs were involved in this – or something similar. Then an unfortunate accident (a mid-air) meant we withdrew on safety grounds.  From that point the BCC stagnated somewhat, until under new management it’s been revised and is now well organised and run. However, it remains very much a southern clubs affair as Skywings reports illustrate.

I don’t know if there is any DHPC interest in being involved or if we could even form a team. I simply pose the idea for discussion, I have no view either way. Further information on the BCC can be found at   https://www.advance-bcc.uk/

LCC  (Lakes Charity Classic) 

This is still in the planning stage and under fresh leadership. It’s scheduled for 19th – 22nd June. Some changes are planned for 2025 that may affect entry re the CP/XC ruling in the B comp and also the X Lakes format.  Information, as it appears can be found at https://www.cumbriasoaringclub.co.uk/lcc/CSC_LCCMain.php

Northern Challenge Series 2025  

This season long, set of standing tasks opens on 1st February. All the information, rules, various downloads and links to tasks have been updated and are available at          https://www.xcflight.com/northern-challenge-series-2025/

The  main change is highlighted and explained in detail – it is now Pilot rated only.

X Dales ’25

This will also be Pilot rated only, but as it’s scheduled for  September there is plenty of time to get P rated.

XCLeague 2025

Ever popular. We will shortly be paying our ‘Club’ entry fee for unlimited pilot entry. The Pilot rating ruling will be enforced this year.  Any CP can submit an xc to their own club league, but will require evidence of a signed off Declaration accompanying their flight.  I believe the homepage is still being updated and there may be a few changes before 1st March        https://www.xcleague.com/xc/leagues/view-1.html

X Contest 2025

I’m not really a user or a fan.  I’ll provide the link and it’s up to you.

https://www.xcontest.org/united-kingdom/

I’m sorry this is a bit rushed. I have some ideas for simple, club comp days which I can use my SC rating to brief and sign off, but they are thoughts I’m still playing with so I’ll leave that for now.

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

To quote Gary Player the golfer,

“The more I practice, the luckier I get”

Get good at the basics before moving on to a Higher performance wing.

After getting competent at ground handling, takeoffs and landings perhaps thermalling could be next

Gaining a good height and being able to get into and out of thermals.

Enjoy flying. We are a privileged generation to fly as we do.

Happy new year one and all. Let's hope 2025 is a good year.

Stephen Craven.
